speaker: interviewer
text: You were telling me about anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: After the revolution--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the revolution?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the-- before the revolution, many people from Tokaj have already escaped already. Because it wasn't no future to stay there. The reason we don't escape before-- Morton have the business. And here is the picture from his store. And the Russian take over. And we was hoping-- because my sister sended the affidavit to come legally. We had the passport, but we don't get the visa. When the children born, we-- we had to pay 500 for and put their name in the passport. But after the revolution, it was I think November 4 or 5. I could remember one day wasn't Russian there. And somebody in Tokaj feel like that he going to be second Hitler-- and was couple names on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So across the street, our family doctor lived. And we was a very good friend. And she came over if we want to sleep in their house, or where my younger sister lived next to her door. And we slept together, because we afraid. So that's make the decision. Then Morton went to Budapest to find out if we could get the passport.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the good friend of us said, if we can arrange to go other side to the country, then she have some way to take us to the border. And now, I don't know if Morton said to you he was the manager in a store. And I was substitute him. But it was terrible. And he came home. I was cooking with candlelight the schmaltz for-- because I get the monthly paycheck. I needed-- and if I don't work same day, they choose the children. So anyhow, he came home from Budapest. He said, we going to escape. And I told my sister. And we went-- we needed to what you can tell a six year old child. We get dressed nicely. And we took a-- a friend of us to cross to the other city, because we don't want from Tokaj in the middle of the night-- and my sister and her child, the baby, and us, and a friend of us. And-- so we went to Budapest with two skirt on. And my third sister, her husband, and the mother came. And we went to [NON-ENGLISH]. To-- meanwhile, Morton-- everybody needed their red book, you know, identification. And Morton had the vacation-- you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stay in [NON-ENGLISH] two night. And it was December 8-- or something it was. And we clothed the-- and even the children needed to sit down to play the piano and the violin, because it was a birthday or something, you know-- with not only Jewish people it were.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then all three of our sister went a different wagon-- I mean, automobile-- to the border. And-- and how we get through, I don't know. But we get through to the border, Marta carrying the little violin of Henry, and Henry and Morton violin. And we arrived to Linz-- a lot of anti-Semitism. And then Morton went to the Joint-- I don't know if he told you-- and told them what happened in this barrack.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle of the night, Hungarian people talk about they came back more people, and probably now escaped, and bring their gold-- and, you know, young-- not educated people. But we was afraid-- like came a priest and bring some orange and chocolate. And one of the young mothers said, Ilona, why don't you come with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, my goodness sake. I survived Auschwitz like a Jew. Now I should go America with a Catholic? Or-- so Morton went to the Joint and told them what happened. And we came with us, not a relative, a young Jewish girl who these people who give-- rescue us. They asked to take with her. So anyhow-- and they said they going to send a automobile who-- who want to tell the Jewish took us by [NON-ENGLISH]-- and the nicest city. I one day I want to go back. The Swedish people sponsor it. And then we went to-- they was there two weeks-- and Salzburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was terrible-- also was a separate Jewish thing-- and needed to stand in line for a little food. And I volunteered to the Red Cross and finally came. Then we going to get the visa, and took us to [NON-ENGLISH] Harbor and the last general work there-- ship. And again, I volunteered to work at-- the first American dollar, I made it up there, three days helping at the dining room to carrying tray. But after I get so sick-- and Marta-- then I was-- I don't think I survive. Then we arrived, actually, February 14, Camp Kilmer-- I mean, Camp Kilmer, you know, in New York harbor-- but needed to wait to let us in. And then I feel good so here. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see the Statue of Liberty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the feeling?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was singing the two hymn, and everybody was happy. And-- and-- and we went out to the ship. And they want to take a movie picture. So Marta have two nice braid. And the Swedish people in-- in the Red Cross, the medical student, give to her a white fur hat and little white coat. And my older sister was waiting at the boat. And tell English-- then let us to go. But she couldn't-- we couldn't hug each other yet, you know. They took us to Camp Kilmer. Then she came with her girlfriend and my cousin to visit us. And the next day, my 13 cousin, who I never met before, they came, and they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long had you not seen your sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the first thing you said to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say to each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What say each other? We couldn't say, [NON-ENGLISH] Hungarian poet-- that is quite a long time ago-- who I like to learn that verse when I was in high school. And the verse said, when go home, old-- all the way to go home was think about what he going to tell the mother. But when they arrived, we just grabbed each other and doesn't say nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] That's what happened. And then my cousin took us out from the Camp Kilmer, because we was afraid we'd get sick again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you start making a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Next day, in my cousin-- understood Hungarian, but doesn't speak. And she had a good friend. Morton get interview. And he was-- he went to work the second day or third day. The next day, her girlfriend came with a green fabric. I don't speak English. I learned in high school English, English, but-- and she said she would like to have a dress. So I had the centimeter. And I took a piece of paper and draw how I would like to make it. And from that on, I have so much work. Then when the time we left, we want to come to California, you know. Then I have so much work. But I went to work once a week in Elizabeth, just to a little bit get up and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get to Pasadena?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938-- August or something-- a year later.

speaker: interviewee
text: '58. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. And then-- And did you work in Pasadena?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I-- we went next day to the temple. And-- and then we stay with my sister four weeks when we get the apartment-- actually a house four blocks below this one. And there was some kind of engineering place that time. And I needed some work. And I was already in Magdeburg, you know, where-- so I talk to one of the engineer-- the easiest way to make money-- two weeks, because then they don't have a to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I saw the newspaper-- fitter. And I don't know what this mean a fitter. Broadway advertise a fitter. So I asked my sister, what this mean a fitter? So she said, just to fit in-- just only need someone just to fit. And I went there. They hired me. And did you always work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I went-- I went back to school in Pasadena City College. We went back to school to get America citizen. Same time when we get the American citizenship, I get my teacher credential teaching adult education. And then I start to teach. And I-- and I taught Pasadena City College over 20 years. And after I retired I still teaching, or taught in a senior center-- volunteer-- and many volunteer-- and hope I will able to teach and tell to the girls the 21st century by heart, how you change the pattern. And I did a collection-- learning never end. That's I believe it. Because that's one of the most important.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jacob Winik-- the Hebrew name [NON-ENGLISH]; and Sophie Winik-- her Hebrew name [NON-ENGLISH]; and Samuel [NON-ENGLISH] Fuchs; and she named-- Samuel was my father; then have a Miriam [NON-ENGLISH] Fuchs after Morton younger sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why did you want to tell your story today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then many people think never happened-- the Holocaust. We have to tell, we who survived, then that's happened. You can not just make up a story like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because somehow we have faith. And somehow we hope my father survive. And I don't know. Just was time, many times, when we think we never survive. And we don't care-- was time, when really, we don't care if we survive or not survive. Is-- is wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about the Holocaust often?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, very often. Like I said, one of the young high school girl asked me to interview. Naturally, I don't told the whole story. I said, if I could paint, I will paint at home Mengele's stand over there-- and life or death. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what message would you like to leave to the generations to come?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next generation to come, they shouldn't wasting time little things. The today young people-- marriage, or little things-- making such a big deal. They should save their energy when have to make a quick big decision. And also, we, the Jewish people, should belong to Jewish organization-- doesn't matter reform, a conservative, or orthodox, or Chabad, because you only from the Jewish organization, you would hear what happening with the Jewish people. And they should study whatever they want to, but should have something then they able to make a living. And they should learn the Shema.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anything else you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: We hope and pray never happen again. And I hope the young people learn from our story, our experience what happened. And they would make every effort to be a good person-- help each other. And- and it doesn't matter if they're not so religious, but should be a good people who believe themself and-- and to help the other person who is not so strong like he or she is. And study hard, because whatever you learn, that stay with you. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we hope never, ever again-- and the Israel should leave-- and no more war. I would like to tell the manufacturer that they shouldn't make children toys-- revolver-- or a-- how they call-- the gun, because I'm very upset when I see a gun in a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Fuchs, would you please introduce your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: My loving husband-- 50 years and 11 and a 1/2 months-- and we're very happily married-- her-- his name is Morton Fuchs. He's a grandfather for four lovely grandchildren. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Fuchs, would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Ilona is my wife almost 51 years. And she's the most wonderful person in my life. And we have two wonderful children and four wonderful grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I forgot to tell you-- really two wonderful children-- we fortunate to have them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we have a nice life. We had always a nice life whether it was when the worst time-- we were really worked hard and struggled. But thank goodness, we are retired now. We have a nice retirement life. We join our children and grandchildren. And we hope for many, many more years in good health--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --where God helps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something to close the interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: What can I say? Marriage only work if you give-- if not only take, but you give too-- give an take. And many young people doesn't realize only way a marriage work. And we work together all my life since 13 year old. I work, and after we can married, we help each other-- even we have the business, I went in, and here in America also. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, my late father, Engel Salomon. He used to introduce himself, [NON-ENGLISH] Engel Salomon. They-- he died in Auschwitz, 1944. We lived in Tokaj.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944. Erev Shavuot, we arrived to Auschwitz. Then I never saw him again. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is my mother, Engel Salomon, nee. [NON-ENGLISH], Flora. from Tokaj. The picture take it approximately 1940 in Tokaj. I get the picture from my sister who was here in America already. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The first sister on the left, Elizabeth. And she was with me in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last-- Elizabeth Scharman. She is a widow. The next one, myself, Ilona, and I was in Auschwitz; the next one at the middle, my older sister, Renee.

speaker: interviewer
text: Last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rosenblum. The next one, Margit Klein-- these two sister was in America from 1939. Oh, excuse me, I did a mistake. Next one is Renee, my younger sister, Charlotte, who was with me in Auschwitz; and next to her, Margit.

speaker: interviewer
text: The last one to the right-- which one is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The last one, Margit Klein. But before her is Charlotte Cohen. And she is the youngest sister. And she was with me in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was the picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: April in New York-- my oldest sister son's bar mitzvah. After 20 years we don't-- first time we was together.

speaker: interviewer
text: The year-- yes. Because you were married that day. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is our wedding picture-- my husband at the right--

speaker: interviewer
text: His name.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Fuchs [NON-ENGLISH]-- here in America, Morton Fuchs-- and myself, Ilona Engel, the last name Fuchs-- taken 1946, February 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tokaj.
